content.text: (update + insert) is an idempotent write operation: if a record with the given ID already exists in the index, the old vector and metadata are replaced with the new values; if the ID does not exist, a new entry is created.  This single semantic eliminates the need for separate insert/update code paths and makes re-indexing pipelines safe to retry without creating duplicates.  Upserts are the standard write operation for keeping a live vector index in sync with a changing document corpus.

attrs.alt: Upsert decision flow (left): client sends id/vector/metadata; if id exists the old vector is replaced, if not a new entry is created; the id is stable in both cases.  Cross-database comparison table (right): Pinecone and Chroma have a native .upsert() method; Weaviate has .replace()/.update(); Milvus requires a delete+insert workaround; FAISS has no built-in upsert.

content.text: In a traditional relational database an upsert is
.  In a vector database the same idea applies to the embedding record: the primary key is a stable string ID (e.g.
), and the payload is the embedding vector plus a metadata dictionary.

Upserts are necessary because real document corpora change.  When a blog post is edited, its embedding must reflect the new content.  When a product description changes price or availability, the metadata must be updated so filters return the right results.  Without upserts you must either delete-then-insert (two operations, potentially non-atomic) or maintain separate logic to detect whether an ID already exists before writing.
The key properties of a correct upsert:
calling upsert twice with the same (id, vector, metadata) produces the same state as calling it once.
the primary key does not change.  Any external system holding a reference to the ID (e.g. a BM25 index, a relational FK) remains valid.
the old vector is replaced by the new one in a single logical operation.  Milvus and FAISS require manual two-step workarounds that are not atomic.
most vector databases replace both the vector and the metadata on upsert.  A separate partial-update API (Weaviate's
) is needed if you want to change only metadata.
is the primary write method -- the docs do not even expose a separate insert.  It accepts a list of
tuples and atomically replaces any existing record with the same id.
Upserts are immediately reflected in queries for serverless indexes.  For pod-based indexes there can be a brief propagation delay to replicas (typically under one second).
Weaviate offers three write semantics through its Python client v4 API:
(partial update -- merges supplied properties into the existing object without touching omitted ones).

import weaviate, weaviate.classes as wvc
import uuid

client = weaviate.connect_to_local()
articles = client.collections.get('Article')

# Deterministic UUID from a stable string key
doc_uuid = str(uuid.uuid5(uuid.NAMESPACE_DNS, 'doc-42'))

# Full upsert: replace entire object if uuid exists, else insert
# Weaviate v4 batch context manager
with articles.batch.dynamic() as batch:
    batch.add_object(
        properties={'title': 'HNSW intro', 'body': '...updated text...', 'version': 2},
        uuid=doc_uuid,         # deterministic -- same doc always gets same uuid
        vector=[0.2, 0.5, ...],  # omit to let the vectorizer re-embed 'body'
    )

# Partial metadata update only -- does NOT re-embed
articles.data.update(
    uuid=doc_uuid,
    properties={'version': 3},  # only 'version' changes; 'title' and 'body' unchanged
)

# Full object replace -- wipes all unspecified fields
articles.data.replace(
    uuid=doc_uuid,
    properties={'title': 'HNSW intro v3', 'body': '...new text...', 'version': 3},
)

client.close()

Milvus does not provide a native atomic upsert for the general case -- the delete-then-insert pattern is the correct production approach.  This means there is a brief window between the delete and the insert during which the ID is not searchable.  For most RAG workloads this is acceptable; for strict consistency requirements, use a database with native upsert support.

from pymilvus import MilvusClient
import numpy as np

client = MilvusClient(uri='http://localhost:19530')
client.load_collection('articles')

def upsert_milvus(collection_name, id_value, vector, metadata):
    """Atomic-ish upsert: delete existing id, then insert new record."""
    # Delete by primary key (id field name: 'doc_id')
    client.delete(
        collection_name=collection_name,
        filter=f'doc_id == "{id_value}"',
    )
    # Insert the new record
    client.insert(
        collection_name=collection_name,
        data=[{
            'doc_id': id_value,
            'embedding': vector,
            'title': metadata.get('title', ''),
            'version': metadata.get('version', 1),
        }],
    )

# Usage
upsert_milvus(
    'articles',
    'doc-42',
    np.random.randn(1536).tolist(),
    {'title': 'HNSW intro v2', 'version': 2},
)

# Batch upsert with bulk delete + bulk insert (faster than one-by-one)
ids_to_replace = ['doc-1', 'doc-2', 'doc-3']
id_list_str = ', '.join(f'"{x}"' for x in ids_to_replace)
client.delete('articles', filter=f'doc_id in [{id_list_str}]')
client.insert('articles', data=new_records)  # list of dicts
but replaces existing records atomically.  It accepts the same four parallel arrays:

import chromadb

client = chromadb.PersistentClient(path='./chroma_db')
collection = client.get_or_create_collection('docs')

# Initial insert
collection.add(
    ids=['doc-42'],
    documents=['HNSW builds a layered graph for ANN search.'],
    metadatas=[{'version': 1, 'source': 'ml-blog'}],
)

# Upsert -- replaces the above record because 'doc-42' exists
collection.upsert(
    ids=['doc-42'],
    documents=['HNSW builds a layered proximity graph. Updated for v2.'],
    metadatas=[{'version': 2, 'source': 'ml-blog'}],
)

# Batch upsert -- ids that exist are replaced; new ids are inserted
collection.upsert(
    ids=['doc-42', 'doc-43', 'doc-44'],
    documents=['...', '...', '...'],
    metadatas=[{'version': 2}, {'version': 1}, {'version': 1}],
)

print(collection.count())  # always reflects the current unique-id count
FAISS has no upsert concept because it has no ID lookup table -- it returns sequential integer offsets, not string IDs.  The common workarounds are:
collect all vectors including the updated one, create a new index from scratch, and swap it in.  Simplest correctness guarantee; expensive for large indexes.
mark the old integer ID as invalid in your ID map, add the new vector with a new integer ID, and periodically compact (rebuild) when the fraction of tombstoned entries exceeds a threshold.
LangChain's FAISS VectorStore maintains a docstore dict alongside the FAISS index and exposes
.  This gives you string IDs and metadata but still requires periodic rebuilds for true updates.

Individual upsert calls carry a fixed per-call overhead: network round-trip, authentication, and request parsing.  Batching amortises this cost.  The optimal batch size is the largest batch that keeps per-batch latency within an acceptable bound -- typically 100–1,000 items for cloud APIs.

import math, time
from pinecone import Pinecone

def batch_upsert(index, records, batch_size=200, namespace=''):
    """Upsert records in batches with progress reporting."""
    n_batches = math.ceil(len(records) / batch_size)
    total_upserted = 0

    for i in range(n_batches):
        batch = records[i * batch_size : (i + 1) * batch_size]
        t0 = time.perf_counter()
        index.upsert(vectors=batch, namespace=namespace)
        elapsed = time.perf_counter() - t0
        total_upserted += len(batch)
        print(f'Batch {i+1}/{n_batches}: {len(batch)} vectors in {elapsed:.2f}s '  
              f'({len(batch)/elapsed:.0f} vec/s)')

    return total_upserted

# Parallel batching with a thread pool (Pinecone / Chroma are safe to call
# concurrently from multiple threads)
from concurrent.futures import ThreadPoolExecutor

def parallel_upsert(index, records, batch_size=200, max_workers=4):
    batches = [
        records[i:i+batch_size] for i in range(0, len(records), batch_size)
    ]
    with ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=max_workers) as pool:
        futures = [pool.submit(index.upsert, vectors=b) for b in batches]
        for f in futures:
            f.result()  # raises if the call failed
    print(f'Upserted {len(records)} vectors in {len(batches)} batches')

For Milvus, avoid parallel delete+insert from separate threads on the same collection -- concurrent deletes and inserts on overlapping IDs can cause inconsistent intermediate states.  Process Milvus batches sequentially.

The most important design decision for safe upserts is how to assign vector IDs.  If IDs are random (e.g. UUID v4), re-indexing the same document creates a new record instead of replacing the old one, silently duplicating it.  The solution is to derive IDs deterministically from the document content or a stable external key.

import hashlib

def doc_id(source_url: str, chunk_index: int) -> str:
    """Stable, unique ID for a chunk derived from its source and position."""
    key = f'{source_url}::chunk-{chunk_index}'
    return hashlib.sha256(key.encode()).hexdigest()[:16]

def content_hash_id(text: str) -> str:
    """ID that changes when text changes -- forces re-embedding on edit."""
    return 'h-' + hashlib.sha256(text.encode()).hexdigest()[:16]

# Example: chunked document pipeline
def index_document(url: str, chunks: list[str], embeddings, collection):
    ids = [doc_id(url, i) for i in range(len(chunks))]
    metadatas = [{'source': url, 'chunk': i} for i in range(len(chunks))]

    # Upsert -- safe to call every time the pipeline runs
    collection.upsert(
        ids=ids,
        documents=chunks,
        embeddings=embeddings,
        metadatas=metadatas,
    )
    # If the document now has fewer chunks than before, delete the orphaned IDs
    # (the caller must track the previous chunk count externally)
Chunk count changes require explicit deletes

If a document had 5 chunks and is re-indexed with only 3 chunks, upsert will correctly replace chunks 0-2 but leave stale chunks 3 and 4 in the index.  Track the previous chunk count and delete orphaned IDs after each re-index.  This is a common source of stale retrieval results.
A full upsert replaces the entire record including the embedding vector.  Re-embedding is expensive -- for OpenAI
a batch of 1,000 documents costs roughly $0.13.  When only metadata changes (e.g. a price field, a view count, a publication date), you can update metadata in-place without re-embedding.
Yes -- embedding recomputed from new text

# Pinecone: update only metadata, keep the existing vector
index.update(
    id='doc-42',
    set_metadata={'price': 49.99, 'in_stock': True},
)

# Chroma: update only metadata (collection.update, not upsert)
collection.update(
    ids=['doc-42'],
    metadatas=[{'version': 3, 'updated_at': '2025-01-01'}],
)

# Weaviate: partial property update
articles.data.update(
    uuid=doc_uuid,
    properties={'price': 49.99, 'in_stock': True},
)
# Note: Weaviate .update() is truly partial -- unspecified properties are preserved
After an upsert is acknowledged by the server, there is often a brief delay before the new vector is fully searchable.  This delay depends on the database:
typically < 1 second.  The gRPC upsert response confirms durability.  Query immediately after upsert may occasionally miss the freshest vector.
replicated to all pod replicas within 1-2 seconds.  Read your own writes is not guaranteed across replicas.
objects are immediately available via the REST API after the batch context manager flushes.  HNSW index construction is asynchronous; new vectors are appended to HNSW within milliseconds.

inserted data is buffered in memory (growing segment) and becomes searchable almost immediately (requires the collection to be loaded).  Flush is triggered automatically; compaction runs in the background.
in-memory and persistent modes are synchronous.  Data is available immediately after upsert returns.

For most RAG use cases, eventual consistency within 1-2 seconds is acceptable because retrieval latency itself is 50-200 ms.  If your application requires immediate read-your-own-writes semantics (e.g. a user edits a document and immediately searches for it), add a short sleep or use the database's query-after-write consistency API.
Using random UUIDs for chunk IDs.
UUID v4 is non-deterministic.  Re-indexing the same document creates duplicate entries.  Use UUID v5 (namespace + stable key) or a hash of (source_url, chunk_index) instead.
Forgetting to delete orphaned chunks after document shrinks.
If a 10-chunk document is updated to 7 chunks, chunks 7-9 remain in the index as stale ghosts.  Track previous chunk counts and clean up.
A full upsert with re-embedding when only a price field changed wastes embedding API tokens.  Use the database's partial update API when only metadata changes.
Between the delete and insert there is a window where the ID is absent from the index.  For latency-sensitive applications, prefer Pinecone or Chroma for atomic upserts.

Pinecone enforces a 2 MB payload limit per upsert call.  Sending 1,536-dim float32 vectors: each vector is 6 KB, so a batch of 350 vectors is already 2.1 MB.  Stay below 200-300 vectors per call for high-dim embeddings.
Not handling duplicate IDs in the same batch.
If a batch contains two records with the same id, behavior is database-specific.  Pinecone uses the last occurrence; Chroma raises an error.  Deduplicate by id before calling upsert.
Derive vector IDs deterministically from a stable key (source URL + chunk index).  This makes the entire indexing pipeline idempotent and safe to retry.
Prefer native upsert APIs (Pinecone, Chroma, Weaviate .add_object()) over delete+insert where available.  Native upserts are atomic and simpler.
Separate the 'is content new?' check from the write.  Hashing the document text and storing the hash in metadata lets you skip re-embedding entirely when the text has not changed.
For metadata-only changes use partial update APIs to avoid unnecessary embedding API calls.  All major databases expose a dedicated metadata update method.
Track chunk counts per document externally (e.g. in a SQLite table or Redis key).  After re-indexing, delete any chunk IDs beyond the new chunk count.
In Milvus, batch deletes and inserts together (bulk delete expr + bulk insert) rather than one pair of calls per document.  This reduces the non-atomic window and is significantly faster.
Test your upsert pipeline with a document that goes from N chunks to N-2 chunks.  The orphan-chunk bug is the most common correctness failure in RAG pipelines.

A vector upsert inserts the record if the ID is new and replaces it if the ID already exists.  This idempotent write is the correct primitive for keeping a live vector index in sync with a changing corpus.
Pinecone and Chroma provide native .upsert(); Weaviate provides .add_object() which upserts by uuid; Milvus requires a non-atomic delete+insert workaround; FAISS has no upsert concept.
Always derive vector IDs deterministically from stable content keys (URL + chunk index, or a content hash).  Random IDs create duplicates on re-indexing.
Track chunk counts per document and delete orphaned chunk IDs when a document shrinks.  Upsert alone cannot remove records that no longer exist in the source.
Use partial update APIs (not full upserts) when only metadata changes -- this avoids unnecessary embedding API calls.
Batch upserts into groups of 100-500 vectors for cloud APIs to balance throughput and payload size.  Stay below 2 MB per call for high-dimensional embeddings.
